speaker: Speaker 1
text: Alright, let's break down the strategy for the upcoming hearing on April 7th. Case GATW4273-26, Daryl Mack versus Greene Consulting Engineers. This is a Con/Arb, which is a high-stakes format. You've got conciliation in the morning, and if no deal is reached, you're straight into arbitration with the same commissioner that afternoon.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It's designed to create maximum pressure, and in this case, the pressure is squarely on the employer. The logistics are set for 11:00 AM at Room 314 in the CCMA Towers in Pretoria. Just a reminder for the record, that's on Pretorius Street, not Francis Baard. It's a common mistake, so getting there by 10:30 is non-negotiable.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Right. Now, the core of the argument is actually quite elegant because the employer's own paperwork does the heavy lifting. Daryl was dismissed after only nine days of employment. He raised concerns about engineering supervision on March 3rd, and by March 9th, he was out. The opening statement needs to be delivered word-for-word to establish that this is an automatically unfair dismissal under Section 187 of the LRA.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: The smoking gun here is the termination letter. If you look at Point 3 and Point 5, they've basically trapped themselves. Point 3 admits the reason for dismissal was Daryl's assertion regarding the Engineering Profession Act, while Point 5 claims a specific person was the supervisor when Point 3 says someone else was. It's a fatal internal contradiction. You don't even have to argue it; you just hand the document to the commissioner and let it speak.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And then there's the procedural failure. No written charges, no impartial chairperson, just a series of meetings where the decision had clearly already been made. When it comes to the money, the opening demand is 219,000 Rand net of tax. That’s about six months of remuneration. The absolute floor is 109,500, which is three months. If they offer a cent less, the instruction is to walk into arbitration.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: There's also a 'live weapon' for the cross-examination. It's the sign-off admission. If Floris or Larry claims there was active supervision, the question is whether they told Daryl that supervision would just be a 'sign-off' on work. In engineering, an administrative sign-off is not the same as active supervision. That distinction is huge.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: The strategy for the cross-examination is surgical. Ask Larry if he ever reviewed a single calculation or assigned a project in those nine days. The answer will be no. Ask Floris who the independent chairperson was. There wasn't one. It's about letting their own silence or admissions confirm the procedural and substantive failures.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Finally, the golden rules for the day. Correct any false premises in their questions, answer and then stop talking, and most importantly, never sign a settlement on the day. Even if the deal looks good, take 48 hours to review it away from the pressure of the hearing room.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Stay calm, stick to the documents, and remember the core framing. This isn't a case of a difficult employee; it's about a professional raising a legitimate regulatory concern and being silenced for it. We'll see how it plays out in Pretoria. Good luck.
